title: Writing - Part 1: Income Inequality
description: Your class has listened to a talk on income inequality and the social, economic and political consequences of the growing gap between the highest and lowest earners in modern societies.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the aspects in your notes. You should explain which aspect you consider most damaging to society, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: When an individual's life outcomes are determined primarily by the economic circumstances of their birth rather than by their own abilities and efforts, the foundational principle of meritocracy on which many societies claim to be built is exposed as a fiction.

Research across multiple countries consistently demonstrates that greater income inequality correlates strongly with worse health outcomes — including lower life expectancy, higher rates of mental illness and increased infant mortality — even after controlling for absolute levels of poverty.

High levels of economic inequality tend to concentrate political influence in the hands of the wealthy, undermining genuine democratic representation and reducing the capacity of governments to implement redistributive policies that might otherwise enjoy broad popular support.

structure.introduction: Introduce the issue of income inequality and indicate the two aspects you will examine.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first aspect, discussing its causes and consequences for individuals and society.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second aspect and argue why you regard it as the most damaging consequence of income inequality.
structure.conclusion: Consolidate your argument and reaffirm which aspect presents the most serious long-term threat.

content.modelAnswer:
Rising income inequality has become one of the defining features of advanced economies over the past four decades, with the gap between the highest and lowest earners continuing to widen in many countries despite periods of sustained economic growth. Among its wide-ranging consequences, the erosion of social mobility and the undermining of democratic representation are both particularly serious.

The decline of genuine social mobility is perhaps inequality's most socially corrosive effect. When the quality of education available to children, the professional networks accessible to young adults and the safety net available to those who fail are all determined largely by inherited economic advantage, the concept of a meritocratic society becomes untenable. The result is not merely individual injustice but a collective waste of talent and potential that constrains innovation and economic dynamism across society as a whole.

However, I would argue that the impact of income inequality on democratic representation poses an even more systemic threat. Concentrated wealth translates into concentrated political influence through campaign financing, lobbying activity and media ownership. When policy agendas are shaped disproportionately by the interests of the affluent, governments lose both the motivation and the electoral mandate to pursue redistributive measures. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle in which inequality perpetuates the political conditions that entrench it further, making the problem progressively harder to address through democratic means.

In conclusion, while the destruction of social mobility causes profound individual harm, the capture of democratic processes by concentrated wealth represents a structural threat to society's capacity to address inequality at all.
